narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your LSAT score of 35 and undergraduate GPA of 2.90. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on past students' academic and personal details, estimated an 82.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—law schools use this tool to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning your grades placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year, which strongly signaled potential struggles ahead. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile ranking, also a 1 compared to the typical student's midpoint around 5 or 6, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance carrying over. While your LSAT score of 35, slightly below the average of 36.7, did help pull the prediction somewhat toward success, it wasn't enough to offset those low decile rankings. Overall, your situation compares to typical students by having average-ish test scores and background but significantly weaker high school grade deciles, which the model sees as the biggest red flags for bar exam outcomes. These top three elements—Year 3 deciles first, then Year 1, with LSAT offering minor counterbalance—dominated the decision more than factors like your GPA, family income, or full-time status. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially as someone building from a middle-class foundation without standout advantages. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it above average or addressing those early grade weaknesses through targeted prep courses or community college refreshers to rebuild your academic deciles. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by demonstrating improvement in subsequent applications, so gather strong letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience and reapply next cycle with evidence of growth. Your full-time commitment is a plus that aligns with the vast majority of successful bar passers, so lean into that determination.
